name: Chrissy Houlahan
tweet: After transferring to a university in NYC, she graduated college in 1965, and published her first short story just two years later. After marrying a Jewish man and moving to Alabama, they were both harassed and threatened because of their interracial marriage. /4

name: Chrissy Houlahan
tweet: "I was ordered immediately to the back of the bus b/c a white woman complained. I had been writing poetry since I was 9, but I realized I would never have the luxury of only writing poetry. I would have to be politically active in order to achieve enough freedom to write at all."

name: Chrissy Houlahan
tweet: Born this day in 1944, Walker was the daughter of a sharecropper in the Jim Crow South. Defying norms, her mother enrolled her in school. Years later, as valedictorian, she earned a scholarship to Spelman College in Atlanta. But her arrival wasn't welcomed by all. /2

name: Chrissy Houlahan
tweet: Douglass’ mere presence put him at risk because he was (at this time) an escaped slave, not a freed man. Being caught could lead to severe physical punishment and a return to slavery, or death by lynch mob. In 1895, Douglass returned to West Chester to speak once again. /4

name: Chrissy Houlahan
tweet: Also present was Dr. Edwin Fussell, founder of the Women’s Medical College of Philadelphia and son of Dr. Bartholomew Fussell, a Chester County native who was one of the greatest conductors of the Underground Railroad, aiding over 200 slaves to freedom. /3

name: Chrissy Houlahan
tweet: Barred from hotel accommodations because he was Black, Douglass was housed by two Quaker sisters who ran a nearby school. At the meeting, he joined fellow abolitionist James Fulton Jr., founder of the East Fallowfield Township Anti-Slavery society. /2

name: Chrissy Houlahan
tweet: As we continue to celebrate #BlackHistoryMonth and its roots in our own community, I'd like to honor the moment in time when known poet and abolitionist, Frederick Douglass, came to West Chester in June of 1844 for an anti-slavery meeting to sway public opinion. /1
